ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper advances a clearly new perspective by formulating dropout as a latent variable model (§3) and introducing the notion of (approximate) expectation-linearity to explicitly quantify the training→inference gap (§4). It proposes a concrete, simple regularizer (sampled expectation-linearization) to control that gap (§5) and derives nontrivial theoretical bounds (Theorems 2–4,5,6) linking network properties, max-norm constraints, and the inference error. Prior works (e.g. ensemble/Bayesian views and Bulò et al. 2016) analyzed or approximated dropout averaging but — per the paper — did not explicitly quantify or regularize this inference gap, so the conceptual and algorithmic contributions are notably novel though building on existing analytic tools.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow (Intro → LVM formulation → expectation-linearity theory → regularizer → experiments → conclusion); the three main contributions are stated explicitly in the Introduction (“We make three contributions…”). Notation is largely consistent and many quantities are defined before use (Sec. 2, Definitions 1–4, Theorems), though the dense mathematical development would benefit from more intuition/examples for readers less comfortable with the formalism. Presentation issues: figures/tables are referenced (Table 1/2, Fig. 1) but are not present in the provided text, and there are duplicated/garbled paragraphs near the end which reduce polish; overall clarity is high but minor editorial fixes would improve readability.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with a sequence of formal definitions, theorems and proofs (Thms. 1–4) and with experiments comparing standard dropout, MC-dropout and a distillation baseline. The mathematical derivations appear largely correct and the proofs are plausible (Jensen argument for Thm.1, Taylor/operator-norm analyses for Thms.2–3, Rademacher-based bound in Thm.4), though several results rely on strong or opaque assumptions (e.g. the unique-best-prediction assumption in Thm.5, unspecified distribution-dependent constants c1,c2) and some bounds are given with distribution-dependent/hidden constants. The experimental methodology uses reasonable baselines and multiple runs, but some reproduction issues are acknowledged, improvements are modest, and no statistical significance tests are reported. Overall the work is technically sound with minor issues (strong assumptions, some unspecified constants and limited statistical analysis) that do not invalidate the main conclusions.
